bytes.topic: Project Setup & Config — zero-config vs explicit config, config file structure
bytes.title: Vite Config — Zero-Config Defaults vs. Explicit vite.config.ts
bytes.body:
Vite works out of the box for React/Vue projects with zero configuration — `vite dev` infers entry point from `index.html`, serves from project root, and applies framework plugins automatically when detected. But the moment you need path aliases, custom build targets, or proxy rules, you drop a `vite.config.ts` at the project root and Vite picks it up automatically via its config resolution order (`vite.config.ts` → `.js` → `.mjs` → `.cjs`).

The config file exports a `UserConfig` object via `defineConfig()` — a typed wrapper that gives you IntelliSense without a separate tsconfig. Key top-level fields: `root` (defaults to `process.cwd()`), `base` (public path, defaults to `/`), `plugins`, `resolve.alias`, `server` (devserver options), and `build` (Rollup passthrough). The `build.rollupOptions` field gives you direct access to Rollup's input/output config for multi-page apps or library mode (`build.lib`). Splitting concerns across `vite.config.base.ts` and environment-specific files is common — merge them with `mergeConfig()` from `vite`.

bytes.topic: Dev Server — HMR, proxy setup, HTTPS in dev
bytes.title: Vite Dev Server — HMR, Proxy Setup, and HTTPS in Dev
bytes.body:
Vite's dev server runs on native ESM, making HMR near-instant — only the changed module and its dependents are re-evaluated, not the full bundle. HMR is enabled by default; you can fine-tune it via `import.meta.hot` to register custom handlers and prevent full-page reloads when state matters.

For API proxying, `server.proxy` in `vite.config.ts` forwards matching paths to your backend. Use `changeOrigin: true` when your backend checks the `Host` header, and `rewrite` to strip path prefixes before forwarding.

For HTTPS in dev — essential when testing secure cookies, HSTS, or WebAuthn — set `server.https: true`. Vite uses `@vitejs/plugin-basic-ssl` for a self-signed cert, or pass a custom `key`/`cert` pair for a trusted local CA (e.g. from `mkcert`). Pair with `server.host: true` to expose on your LAN for device testing.

bytes.topic: Code Splitting — manual chunks, dynamic imports, route-based splitting
bytes.title: Vite Code Splitting — Manual Chunks, Dynamic Imports, and Route-Based Splitting
bytes.body:
Vite's Rollup-based build splits code automatically, but you control the strategy. Dynamic imports create split points instantly — Vite emits a separate chunk per `import()` call with zero config. For route-based splitting in React, wrap lazy-loaded components with `React.lazy` and `Suspense`; Vite handles the chunk boundaries automatically.

For finer control, use `build.rollupOptions.output.manualChunks` in `vite.config.ts`. This lets you group vendor libraries into a stable `vendor` chunk, preventing cache-busting on every app change. You can pass a function instead of an object for dynamic logic — inspect the module ID and return a chunk name.

Avoid over-splitting: too many small chunks increases waterfall requests. Vite's `build.chunkSizeWarningLimit` (default 500 kB) flags bloated chunks. Use `vite-bundle-visualizer` or `rollup-plugin-visualizer` to audit your output. For async routes that share heavy deps like `lodash`, pin them to `manualChunks` so they're fetched once and cached across routes.

bytes.topic: Tree Shaking — how it works, why side effects matter, marking packages as pure
bytes.title: Vite Tree Shaking — How Side Effects Break It and How to Fix Them
bytes.body:
Vite delegates tree shaking to Rollup during `vite build`. Rollup statically analyzes ES module imports and eliminates exports that are never referenced — but only when it can prove a module is side-effect-free. If a package's `package.json` omits `"sideEffects": false`, Rollup conservatively keeps every imported module in the bundle, even if you only use one named export.

This matters most with utility libraries. Importing `import { merge } from 'lodash-es'` is safe — lodash-es ships proper ESM with `"sideEffects": false`. But a library that mutates globals or registers polyfills on import cannot be marked pure.

For your own packages consumed by Vite, set `"sideEffects": false` in `package.json`, or list specific files that do have side effects: `"sideEffects": ["./src/polyfills.js"]`. Inside `vite.config.ts`, you can also pass Rollup's `treeshake` option for fine-grained control — for example, `treeshake: { moduleSideEffects: false }` tells Rollup to assume all modules are pure unless proven otherwise. Verify results with `vite build --reporter verbose` or inspect the Rollup bundle visualizer plugin.

bytes.topic: Bundle Analysis — visualizing size, finding bloat, setting size budgets in CI
bytes.title: Vite Bundle Analysis — Visualizing Size, Finding Bloat, and Setting CI Budgets
bytes.body:
Vite's rollup-based output gives you full control over bundle introspection. Install `rollup-plugin-visualizer` and add it to your `vite.config.ts` plugins array — on build it generates an interactive treemap (`stats.html`) showing every module's contribution to your chunks. Spot bloat fast: a lodash import pulling in 70 KB signals you need `lodash-es` with tree-shaking, or a targeted `lodash.debounce` import instead.

For CI enforcement, Vite exposes `build.chunkSizeWarningLimit` (default 500 KB), but that's just a warning. Real enforcement comes via `vite-bundle-analyzer` or a custom Rollup plugin using the `generateBundle` hook — iterate `bundle` entries, sum `.code.length`, and `throw` if a chunk exceeds your budget. Pair this with `build.rollupOptions.output.manualChunks` to split vendor libraries into predictable named chunks (`vendor-react`, `vendor-charts`) so regressions are immediately obvious in diffs. Run `vite build --mode production` in CI and fail the pipeline on oversized output.

bytes.topic: Asset Handling — images, fonts, SVGs, hashing for cache busting
bytes.title: Vite Asset Handling — Hashing, Inlining, and Cache Busting Done Right
bytes.body:
Vite processes static assets through its asset pipeline, applying content-based hashing to filenames in production builds — `logo.png` becomes `logo.Bx3kW9aP.png`. This hash changes only when file content changes, giving you aggressive CDN caching with automatic invalidation.

Import assets directly in JS/TS to get the resolved, hashed URL at runtime:

```ts
import logoUrl from './assets/logo.png'
```

Files under 4KB are inlined as base64 data URIs by default, eliminating a network round-trip. Control this threshold via `build.assetsInlineLimit` in `vite.config.ts`.

For SVGs, Vite treats them as URLs by default, but with `vite-plugin-svgr` you can import them as React components. Fonts placed in `public/` skip the pipeline entirely — no hashing, no transform — ideal when you need stable URLs for `@font-face` declarations.

The `public/` directory is your escape hatch: files here are copied as-is and referenced with absolute paths like `/favicon.ico`. Everything else goes through the pipeline and gets a content hash.

Customize the output filename pattern with `build.rollupOptions.output.assetFileNames`: `'assets/[name].[hash][extname]'`.

bytes.topic: Environment Variables — .env files, build-time vs runtime, define plugin
bytes.title: Vite Environment Variables — .env Files, Build-Time Injection, and the define Plugin
bytes.body:
Vite exposes env variables to client code only if they're prefixed with VITE_. Files like .env, .env.local, .env.production are loaded automatically via dotenv — but only VITE_* keys land in import.meta.env. Non-prefixed keys stay server-side only.

At build time, Vite statically replaces import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL with its literal string value. This means dead-code elimination works: if (import.meta.env.DEV) branches get stripped in production builds. import.meta.env.MODE, .DEV, .PROD, and .SSR are always available.

For values that can't be known at build time — think feature flags fetched from a config server — use the define plugin option in vite.config.ts. define performs a direct AST-level string replacement, so __APP_VERSION__ becomes '1.2.3' in the output bundle without any runtime lookup.

Critical distinction: import.meta.env values are frozen at build time. If you need true runtime config (e.g. per-deployment API URLs without rebuilding), serve a /config.json and fetch it at app startup instead.

bytes.topic: Source Maps — types, trade-offs in production
bytes.title: Vite Source Maps — Types and Production Trade-offs
bytes.body:
Vite exposes `build.sourcemap` with four modes that behave very differently in production. `true` emits separate `.map` files alongside your chunks — Rollup writes them, browsers load them on-demand, and your bundle size stays lean. `'inline'` base64-encodes the entire map into each chunk, bloating output by 2–3× — never ship this to prod. `'hidden'` generates `.map` files but strips the `//# sourceMappingURL` comment, so browsers ignore them while tools like Sentry can still upload and symbolicate crash reports via `sentry-vite-plugin`. The default is `false`, meaning zero map output.

For most production setups, `'hidden'` is the right call: you get full stack trace symbolication in your error monitoring tool without leaking original source to end users. Pair it with `sentry-vite-plugin`'s `sourcemaps.filesToDeleteAfterUpload` to scrub `.map` files from your deploy artifact post-upload. For internal tools or staging, `true` is fine — just ensure your CDN or server blocks `.map` requests if source privacy matters.

bytes.topic: TypeScript — transpile-only vs type-check, speed vs safety trade-off
bytes.title: Vite TypeScript — Transpile-Only Mode and When to Add Type Checking Back
bytes.body:
Vite never runs `tsc` during dev or build by default. It uses esbuild to strip TypeScript types — pure transpilation, zero type checking. This is why `vite dev` starts in milliseconds regardless of project size. Your type errors are invisible to Vite itself.

To add type safety without sacrificing Vite's speed, run `tsc --noEmit` as a separate process. Wire it into your CI pipeline or as a parallel npm script using `concurrently`. Never block `vite build` on type checking in local dev — let esbuild do what it does fastest.

For the build step specifically, `vite build` will happily emit broken JS if your types are wrong. The idiomatic fix is a `build` script that runs `tsc --noEmit && vite build` — TypeScript gates the emit, Vite handles the bundling. You can also use `vite-plugin-checker` to surface type errors as HMR overlays during dev without blocking the server startup. Configure it with `typescript: true` and it spawns a background `tsc` worker.

bytes.topic: CSS Processing — PostCSS, CSS Modules, preprocessor support
bytes.title: Vite CSS Processing — PostCSS, CSS Modules, and Preprocessors Without Extra Config
bytes.body:
Vite has first-class CSS processing built into its transform pipeline — no webpack loaders, no manual plugin wiring. PostCSS runs automatically if a `postcss.config.js` (or `.cjs`) exists at the root; Vite picks it up and applies it to every CSS file it processes, including those inside `<style>` blocks in `.vue` files.

CSS Modules work out of the box for any file named `*.module.css`. Import it as a JS object and Vite generates scoped class names at build time. The `modules` option in `vite.config.ts` under `css.modules` lets you configure `localsConvention`, `generateScopedName`, and more.

For preprocessors — Sass, Less, Stylus — install the preprocessor itself (`npm i -D sass`) and Vite's resolver handles the rest. No `vite-plugin-sass` needed. You can pass preprocessor options globally via `css.preprocessorOptions.scss.additionalData` to inject shared variables into every file without explicit imports.

LightningCSS is available as an opt-in replacement for both esbuild's CSS minifier and PostCSS, enabled via `css.transformer: 'lightningcss'` in Vite 4.4+.

bytes.topic: Aliasing & Path Resolution — @ aliases, module resolution config
bytes.title: Vite Path Aliases — Clean Imports with @ and resolve.alias
bytes.body:
Vite's `resolve.alias` in `vite.config.ts` lets you replace deep relative imports with short, memorable prefixes. The canonical pattern maps `@` to your `src` directory, but you can define as many aliases as your project needs — `@components`, `@hooks`, `@lib`, etc.

Under the hood, Vite passes aliases to Rollup's resolver during build and to its dev-server module graph at runtime — so the same config works in both modes without extra plugins.

One gotcha: TypeScript doesn't know about Vite aliases. You must mirror every alias in `tsconfig.json` under `compilerOptions.paths`, or imports will typecheck as missing modules. The `vite-tsconfig-paths` plugin automates this by reading `tsconfig.json` directly so you maintain a single source of truth.

For monorepos, `resolve.alias` also accepts a function `(id) => string | undefined`, letting you dynamically redirect workspace package imports to local source files instead of built dist artifacts — critical for fast HMR across packages.

bytes.topic: Performance — build speed, caching, parallel processing, incremental builds
bytes.title: Vite's Dependency Pre-Bundling — Why Your First Dev Start Is Slow (And Every Restart Isn't)
bytes.body:
Vite splits build work into two phases: pre-bundling bare module imports using esbuild, and serving your own source files as native ESM. The first `vite dev` run scans your entry points, bundles all `node_modules` dependencies into single ESM chunks under `node_modules/.vite/deps/`, and writes a metadata hash. Every subsequent start checks that hash — if your `package.json`, lockfile, or `vite.config.ts` hasn't changed, the cache is reused and pre-bundling is skipped entirely.

To force a full re-bundle without deleting the cache manually, run `vite --force`. To pre-bundle specific packages that Vite misses (e.g. CJS-heavy transitive deps), use `optimizeDeps.include`. Conversely, packages that Vite incorrectly bundles together can be excluded via `optimizeDeps.exclude`.

For build performance in production, `build.rollupOptions.output.manualChunks` lets you split vendor code deliberately, reducing re-bundling across deploys when only app code changes — Rollup's cache invalidation is hash-based per chunk.

bytes.topic: Production Optimization — minification, compression (gzip/brotli), dead code elimination
bytes.title: Vite Production Optimization — Minification, Compression, and Tree-Shaking
bytes.body:
Vite uses esbuild for minification by default (`build.minify: 'esbuild'`), which is fast but skips some advanced optimizations. Switch to `'terser'` for deeper compression — it handles complex patterns esbuild won't touch, at the cost of slower builds. For compression, Vite doesn't serve gzip/brotli directly — that's your static server's job — but `vite-plugin-compression` generates `.gz` and `.br` artifacts at build time so Nginx or a CDN can serve them with zero runtime overhead.

Tree-shaking is automatic via Rollup, but it only eliminates truly unreferenced ESM exports. Side-effectful imports survive unless you mark your package `"sideEffects": false` in `package.json`. For library code, use `build.lib` mode with `formats: ['es']` to produce a pure ESM bundle that downstream bundlers can tree-shake. You can also use `build.rollupOptions.treeshake` to tune granularity — set `preset: 'safest'` for aggressive elimination or `'recommended'` for a balanced default. Use `rollup-plugin-visualizer` to audit what actually ships.

bytes.topic: Plugin System — how plugins extend the build pipeline
bytes.title: Vite Plugin System — How Plugins Extend the Build Pipeline
bytes.body:
Vite's plugin system is a superset of Rollup's, meaning any Rollup plugin works in Vite with minimal changes. Plugins are plain objects exposing lifecycle hooks — Vite adds its own hooks on top for dev-server-specific behavior.

Two categories matter: **universal hooks** (run during both dev and build) like `transform`, `resolveId`, and `load`; and **Vite-specific hooks** like `configureServer`, `transformIndexHtml`, and `handleHotUpdate`. The `enforce` property controls ordering — `'pre'` runs before Vite core plugins, `'post'` runs after.

The `transform` hook is the workhorse: it receives a module's code and id, and returns transformed code plus an optional source map. Vite pipes every imported module through the plugin chain in order. `resolveId` lets you intercept bare specifiers and redirect them to virtual modules — pair it with `load` to serve synthetic content. `configureServer` gives you direct access to the Vite dev server's Connect middleware stack, useful for injecting custom HTTP routes or WebSocket handlers during development.

bytes.topic: Vite-specific — native ESM in dev, esbuild pre-bundling, Rollup in prod
bytes.title: Vite's Dual Engine: Why Dev and Prod Feel Different
bytes.body:
Vite runs two completely different build pipelines depending on environment — and understanding the split prevents production surprises.

In dev, Vite skips bundling entirely. It serves source files as native ES modules directly to the browser, letting the browser's module graph drive what gets loaded. Vite only transforms files on request, making cold starts near-instant regardless of project size. But bare imports like `import _ from 'lodash-es'` would trigger hundreds of network requests — so Vite uses esbuild to pre-bundle node_modules into single ESM files, cached in `node_modules/.vite/deps`.

In prod, esbuild hands off to Rollup. Rollup's superior tree-shaking, code-splitting, and chunk optimization make it the right tool for shipping. This is why you configure `build.rollupOptions` — not an esbuild equivalent — for output chunking, externals, and library mode.

The gap matters: a CJS package that esbuild pre-bundles fine in dev may fail Rollup's ESM-strict prod build. Use `vite build --mode development` to catch these mismatches early, or inspect pre-bundle output via `vite --debug` to see exactly what esbuild resolved.

bytes.topic: Webpack-specific — loaders vs plugins, module federation
bytes.title: Vite for Webpack Users — Loaders, Plugins, and Module Federation Explained
bytes.body:
Vite has no concept of loaders. In Webpack, loaders transform files (babel-loader, css-loader) while plugins hook into compilation lifecycle — two separate systems. Vite collapses both into a single Rollup-compatible plugin API. A Vite plugin can expose `transform()` for file transformation, `resolveId()` for module resolution, and `load()` for custom module loading, covering everything Webpack split across loaders and plugins.

For module federation — runtime code sharing across micro-frontends — Vite uses `@originjs/vite-plugin-federation`. Unlike Webpack's built-in `ModuleFederationPlugin`, this requires `build.target: 'esnext'` since it relies on native ES dynamic imports rather than CommonJS chunks. The `exposes` and `remotes` config keys map directly to Webpack's vocabulary.

Critical gotcha: Vite's dev server runs native ESM but federation remotes must be pre-built. You can't point a host at `vite dev` output — run `vite build --watch` on the remote instead and serve the `dist/` directory. This is the single biggest migration pitfall from Webpack's seamless HMR-based federation.

bytes.topic: Monorepo Support — workspace packages, shared configs, turborepo integration
bytes.title: Vite Monorepos — Workspace Packages, Shared Configs, and Turborepo
bytes.body:
Vite plays well in monorepos when you wire it correctly. Use `resolve.alias` or `optimizeDeps.include` to ensure workspace packages are processed by Vite's pipeline rather than served as pre-bundled CJS artifacts. In a pnpm/npm workspace, sibling packages linked via `workspace:*` are treated as source — not node_modules — so Vite won't pre-bundle them unless you explicitly add them to `optimizeDeps.include`.

For shared Vite configs, export a base config factory from a `@repo/vite-config` package and merge it per-app using `mergeConfig` from `vite`. This keeps plugin arrays, resolve aliases, and test settings DRY across apps.

With Turborepo, define a `build` pipeline that caches `.vite` and `dist` outputs. Set `inputs` to include your shared config package so cache invalidation propagates correctly — a change in `@repo/vite-config` should bust all downstream app builds. Pair this with Vite's `build.watch` mode during local dev for incremental rebuilds across packages without restarting HMR servers.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — config sharing, extending base configs, CI build caching
bytes.title: Vite Config Sharing — Extend Base Configs Across Monorepos and Speed Up CI
bytes.body:
Vite has no built-in `extends` like TSConfig, but `mergeConfig` from `vite` gives you composable, type-safe config inheritance. Define a `vite.base.config.ts` at your repo root with shared plugins (e.g. `@vitejs/plugin-react`, `vite-tsconfig-paths`) and resolve aliases. Each package then imports and merges it, overriding only what differs — `build.outDir`, `server.port`, test globals, etc.

For CI caching, Vite's Rollup-based build writes chunks to `dist/assets`. Cache that directory keyed on a hash of `vite.config.ts`, `package-lock.json`, and your source files. On GitHub Actions, use `actions/cache` with a restore key fallback so a partial cache hit still avoids re-downloading node_modules. Vite's `build.manifest: true` emits `dist/.vite/manifest.json` — commit its hash as a cache buster instead of timestamps.

Depcheck `optimizeDeps.include` in your base config: pre-bundled deps are written to `node_modules/.vite/deps` and reused across runs if the lockfile hash matches, cutting cold-start times from ~4 s to under 500 ms in large apps.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — build time tracking, bundle size regression alerts in CI
bytes.title: Vite Build Monitoring — Track Build Times and Catch Bundle Regressions in CI
bytes.body:
Vite exposes build metadata you can hook into for CI-level monitoring. The `vite-bundle-analyzer` plugin generates stats, but for lightweight regression alerts, lean on Vite's built-in `rollupOptions` and the `generateBundle` hook directly.

Use a custom Vite plugin that records chunk sizes post-build and compares them against a committed baseline (`bundle-baseline.json`). In CI, fail the job if any chunk exceeds its baseline by more than a configurable threshold (e.g. 10%).

For build time tracking, wrap `vite build` with `perf_hooks` or simply use `time` in CI and pipe results to a metrics store. Vite 5+ also emits `vite:buildStart` and `vite:buildEnd` events you can tap via plugin hooks.

Store baselines in version control so every PR diff shows bundle impact. Pair with GitHub Actions' `actions/upload-artifact` to persist `stats.json` across runs for trend analysis. This gives you bundle size history without a paid service.
